sound_type: high sharp "eenk" bark -- a short, loud, piercing alarm call produced from talus slopes at high elevation, the 75th acoustic element, the highest-elevation sound in the engine, E=4, audible at 200-300 meters, alarm function, the alpine sentinel's call
ecosystem: alpine/subalpine talus slopes -- rocky boulder fields above treeline or at the upper edge of subalpine forest, typically at elevations of 1,500-3,000+ meters in the Cascades and Olympics. Talus habitat: broken rock with interstitial spaces used for denning, food caching, and thermoregulation.
season: active spring through fall (May-October); remains active in winter beneath snowpack in talus -- does NOT hibernate

coordinates.description: Reference for alpine talus habitat in the Washington Cascades, Mount Rainier vicinity. The American Pika inhabits rocky talus slopes at and above treeline. The "eenk" bark carries across boulder fields on clear alpine days.

taxonomy_note.description: NEW FAMILY: Ochotonidae (34th). Thirteenth mammalian entry. Eleventh mammalian species. BACK-TO-BACK LAGOMORPHA with Snowshoe Hare (73). Second Lagomorpha, different family (Ochotonidae vs. Leporidae). Lagomorpha families diverged approximately 35-40 million years ago. The American Pika is NOT a rodent -- Lagomorpha and Rodentia diverged approximately 80-90 million years ago.
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Returns to Placentalia after the marsupial (Opossum, 72). The Pika is a placental mammal in order Lagomorpha.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Ochotona from Mongolian ogdoi/ochodona, the local name for pikas in Central Asia. princeps from Latin 'chief' or 'first,' referring to this being the first American pika described scientifically (Richardson, 1828). Ochotona princeps = 'the chief pika.' Common name 'Pika' from Tungusic piika.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Ochotona -- approximately 30 species worldwide, most in Central and East Asia. Only two New World species: O. princeps (American Pika) and O. collaris (Collared Pika, Alaska/Yukon).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Lagomorpha -- SECOND Lagomorpha in the engine after Snowshoe Hare (73, Leporidae). Back-to-back Lagomorpha (73-74) like back-to-back Anatidae (58-59). Contains two families: Leporidae (rabbits and hares) and Ochotonidae (pikas). Both families now represented. The order is NOT Rodentia -- Lagomorpha diverged from Rodentia approximately 80-90 million years ago.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- thirteenth mammalian entry. Eleventh mammalian species. FOUR orders (Rodentia, Carnivora, Didelphimorphia, Lagomorpha). EIGHT families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae, Procyonidae, Canidae, Didelphidae, Leporidae, Ochotonidae). TWO infraclasses (Placentalia, Marsupialia).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Ochotonidae's FIRST entry at degree 74. The 34th family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Fifth new family in six degrees (Mephitidae 69, Procyonidae 70, Canidae 71, Didelphidae 72, Ochotonidae 74). Ochotonidae contains one genus (Ochotona) with approximately 30 species.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Approximately 3,000-10,000 Hz. The bark is a short, sharp, high-pitched vocalization. High frequency for body size.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Approximately 4,000-8,000 Hz (the piercing core of the bark).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. ALARM BARK. Two primary types: (1) Short call -- single sharp 'eenk' alarm (0.1-0.2 sec), given singly or in series of 2-5. (2) Long call -- territorial/mating call, series of linked calls over 1-3 seconds.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Short call: 0.1-0.2 seconds per bark. Long call: 1-3 seconds total. Alarm calling can persist intermittently for minutes when a predator is present.
song_metrics.note_structure: Short call: single sharp impulse, abrupt onset and offset. Long call: series of linked impulses with brief inter-call intervals.
song_metrics.note_types: Eenk (sharp alarm bark), territorial long call (linked series), distress squeal (rare, when captured).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Short call: single or series of 2-5 barks with 1-3 second intervals. Long call: continuous series over 1-3 seconds. No complex phrase structure -- the Pika's vocalization is a simple, repeated, piercing impulse.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp, piercing, metallic -- like a tiny bell struck hard. Clean onset, brief duration, clean offset. No warble or trill. The 'eenk' has a nasal quality. The sound is unmistakable once learned -- no other alpine animal produces anything similar.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Loud relative to body size. Audible at 200-300 meters across open alpine terrain. The Pika is far easier to hear than to see. The 'eenk' bark is the primary indicator of Pika presence for hikers and surveyors.
song_metrics.tempo: Short call: individual barks separated by 1-3 seconds. Long call: rapid series (4-8 calls per second). Overall tempo during alarm calling: moderate, intermittent.
song_metrics.context: ALARM and TERRITORIAL. Short call: given when a predator is detected (weasel, hawk, eagle, human hiker). Functions as personal alert and warning to neighbors. Long call: territorial advertisement and mate attraction, more common during breeding season. The Pika is acoustically active during its surface activity periods (early morning, late afternoon).
song_metrics.regional_variation: Some geographic variation in call structure across subspecies, but the basic 'eenk' bark is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: HIGH SHARP 'EENK' BARK -- the 75th acoustic element. A piercing alarm call from alpine talus slopes. E=4. Audible at 200-300 meters. The highest-elevation sound in the engine. The alpine sentinel's call -- small body, big bark, vast sightlines, rock echo.

physical_description.overall: Small, compact, egg-shaped mammal. 162-216 mm total length (no visible tail). 120-185 grams. The size of a tennis ball with ears. Big round ears, no visible tail, dense fur, short limbs. Adapted for life in alpine talus -- compact body fits between boulders.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 74, the diagnostic features are: (1) COMPACT ROUND BODY -- egg-shaped, no visible tail, tennis-ball size. (2) SHORT ROUND EARS -- 20-26 mm, unlike the long ears of rabbits and hares. (3) NO VISIBLE TAIL -- vestigial tail hidden in fur. (4) DENSE FUR -- grayish-brown to cinnamon, exceptionally thick for alpine insulation. (5) FUR-SOLED FEET -- soles covered with fur for insulation and traction on cold rock. (6) BOULDER PERCHING -- typically seen sitting erect on a prominent talus boulder.
physical_description.fur_detail: DORSUM: Dense, soft, grayish-brown to cinnamon-brown. VENTRAL: Lighter, buff to grayish. Two molts per year: summer pelage shorter and more reddish-brown; winter pelage longer, denser, grayer. Exceptionally dense for body size -- critical insulation for alpine cold. NOT aposematic -- provides some camouflage on brown rock.
physical_description.dental_detail: Lagomorph dentition with four upper incisors (two large, two small 'peg teeth' behind -- the key distinction from rodents, which have only two upper incisors). Fully herbivorous. Practices coprophagy (re-ingestion of cecal pellets for nutrient extraction).
physical_description.field_id: In alpine talus above treeline: a small, round, brown mammal sitting erect on a prominent boulder, delivering a sharp 'eenk' bark. No visible tail. Short round ears. Quick dashes between boulders. Often heard before seen -- the piercing bark carries across open terrain. Distinguished from all other alpine mammals by the combination of tiny size, compact shape, short ears, no visible tail, and characteristic bark.

hay_gathering_note.description: HAY-GATHERING: The Pika's most remarkable behavior. Cuts fresh vegetation (grasses, sedges, wildflowers) and carries cuttings to specific locations on the talus slope. Vegetation is laid out on flat rocks to dry in the sun -- literally making hay. Once dried, moved into talus interior for winter storage. Haypiles can weigh 15-30 kg -- more than 100 times the Pika's body weight. The Pika does NOT hibernate and feeds on stored hay through 6-8 months of alpine winter.
hay_gathering_note.selectivity: Pikas preferentially cache plants with high caloric/nutritional value. Sometimes cache mildly toxic plants whose toxins break down during drying -- a natural preservative that retards spoilage. Sophisticated food quality assessment.
hay_gathering_note.significance: One of the most complex food-storage behaviors in any PNW mammal. The Pika is an alpine farmer -- harvesting, processing (drying), and storing food for future use.

climate_note.description: CLIMATE CHANGE INDICATOR. The Pika is one of the most heat-sensitive mammals in North America. Lethal temperature: 25.5 C (78 F) with extended exposure. Dense alpine fur and high metabolic rate make pikas extremely vulnerable to warming. Primary thermoregulation: behavioral retreat to cool talus interstices.
climate_note.population_losses: Great Basin populations documented losing pikas from low-elevation sites over the past century. Range contracting from lower and southern margins. Washington populations currently relatively secure but warming trends are pushing habitable zone higher.
climate_note.conservation_status: Petitioned for ESA listing in 2010; declined (not range-wide threatened). IUCN Least Concern with climate vulnerability noted. Washington monitors as climate indicator species.
climate_note.escalator_to_extinction: As temperatures warm, pikas retreat upslope. Mountain summits are finite. A pika at the summit has nowhere higher to go. The habitable zone shrinks from below -- the 'escalator to extinction.'

nodes.title: New Family: Ochotonidae -- 34th Family
nodes.description: Ochotonidae enters as the 34th family in the engine. The pika family. Contains one genus (Ochotona) with approximately 30 species. Fifth new family in six degrees. The engine's second Lagomorpha family (after Leporidae at 73).

nodes.title: Back-to-Back Lagomorpha (73-74) -- Like Back-to-Back Anatidae (58-59)
nodes.description: Snowshoe Hare (73, Leporidae) and American Pika (74, Ochotonidae) are back-to-back Lagomorpha. Same order, consecutive degrees, different families. Parallels back-to-back Anatidae (58-59). Forest hare vs. alpine pika. Low elevation vs. highest elevation. Both lagomorph families now represented.

nodes.title: High Sharp 'Eenk' Bark -- 75th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: The Pika's piercing alarm bark. Approximately 3,000-10,000 Hz. Audible at 200-300 meters. E=4. The highest-elevation sound in the engine. Alpine acoustic -- thin air, vast sightlines, rock echo. The loudest mammalian call relative to body size.

nodes.title: Climate Change Indicator -- First Climate-Driven Conservation Narrative
nodes.description: The Pika is the engine's first species whose conservation status is primarily driven by climate warming. Lethal temperature 25.5 C. Populations retreating upslope. Low-elevation extinctions documented. Cannot be saved by local habitat protection alone -- requires global climate action. The escalator to extinction.

nodes.title: Hay-Gathering -- Alpine Farming Behavior
nodes.description: The Pika cuts and dries vegetation for winter storage. Haypiles of 15-30 kg (100+ times body weight). Does not hibernate. Active year-round beneath snowpack. One of the most complex food-storage behaviors in any PNW mammal. Selective foraging with natural preservation techniques.

nodes.title: Highest Elevation Species in the Engine
nodes.description: Alpine talus at 1,500-3,000+ meters. The highest elevation of any species in the engine. Above treeline. Previous species inhabit lower elevations (forest, wetland, suburban). The Pika is the engine's vertical frontier.

nodes.title: Does Not Hibernate Despite Alpine Cold
nodes.description: The Pika remains active year-round beneath the snowpack. Feeds on stored hay through 6-8 months of winter. The snowpack provides insulation. The talus interstices maintain near-freezing temperatures. Active in winter while most alpine life is dormant.

nodes.title: The Poor Moon Parallel
nodes.description: Both exist in gaps -- Poor Moon between Fleet Foxes albums, the Pika between talus boulders. Both small but resonant -- one album, one sharp bark. Both at elevation -- Fleet Foxes company, alpine summits. Both climate-sensitive -- temporal brevity, geographic shrinkage.

nodes.title: Snowshoe Hare Connection (SPS-073)
nodes.description: Back-to-back Lagomorpha. Same order, different families. Forest vs. alpine. Low vs. high elevation. Large vs. tiny. Color change vs. haypile. Thumping vs. barking. Both active in winter, neither hibernates. The Lagomorpha span the elevation gradient.

nodes.title: Short-tailed Weasel Connection (SPS-067)
nodes.description: Predator-prey, alpine overlap. The weasel is the Pika's most dangerous predator -- its slender body can follow the Pika into talus interstices. The predator that breaches the fortress.

nodes.title: Spotted Owl Connection (SPS-004)
nodes.description: Both threatened by large-scale environmental change. Owl: habitat loss and Barred Owl competition. Pika: climate warming. Both indicator species -- their decline signals broader ecosystem degradation. Different scales of threat: regional vs. global.

nodes.title: Thirteenth Mammalian Entry -- Four Orders, Eight Families
nodes.description: Thirteen mammalian entries across fourteen degrees (61-74). Eleven species. Four orders (Rodentia, Carnivora, Didelphimorphia, Lagomorpha). Eight families. Two infraclasses. The mammalian era continues to deepen with genuine taxonomic diversity.

cross_references.relationship: BACK-TO-BACK LAGOMORPHA (73-74). Same order, different families (Leporidae vs. Ochotonidae). Forest vs. alpine. Low vs. high elevation. Large vs. tiny. Color change vs. haypile. Thumping alarm vs. barking alarm. Both active year-round, neither hibernates.

PREDATOR-PREY, ALPINE OVERLAP. The weasel is the Pika's most dangerous predator. Its slender body can follow the Pika into talus interstices -- the predator that breaches the fortress. Both alpine-capable. Different roles: hunter and hunted.

SUBALPINE FOREST NEIGHBOR. The marten occupies subalpine forest; the Pika occupies alpine talus above. Ranges overlap at the forest-alpine transition. Predator-prey where habitats meet. Both cold-adapted. Both habitat-specific.

CONSERVATION PARALLEL. Both conservation-sensitive species monitored by biologists. Fisher: threatened by fragmentation and trapping (local solutions). Pika: threatened by climate warming (global solutions). Different threats, similar vulnerability.

CLIMATE-THREATENED PARALLEL. Both indicator species whose decline signals broader ecosystem degradation. Owl: old-growth forest health. Pika: alpine climate health. Both threatened by large-scale environmental change.
Thirteenth mammalian entry. Eleventh species. Fourth order (Lagomorpha). Eighth family (Ochotonidae). Second Lagomorpha family. Back-to-back with Snowshoe Hare (73).
Alpine talus habitat. Highest elevation species. Climate change indicator -- populations retreating upslope. Hay-gathering behavior. Does not hibernate. The engine's alpine ecological portrait.
